levers.name: Participant Flow Strategy
levers.consequences: This lever governs the balance between participant recruitment speed and participant quality. Direct effect: faster recruitment might compromise selection criteria, while slower recruitment ensures a more curated cohort.
options: Optimize for high-volume participant intake through aggressive marketing campaigns
Prioritize participant quality over quantity by implementing stricter inclusion/exclusion criteria
Employ a hybrid approach combining targeted recruitment with selective screening to strike a balance between speed and quality
levers.review_lever: This lever overlooks the impact of participant dropout rates on data yield.

levers.name: Sensing Tier Strategy
levers.consequences: This lever controls the trade-off between data resolution and participant comfort. Direct effect: escalating to enhanced nights can disrupt natural sleep patterns, while maintaining low-burden monitoring might compromise data quality.
options: Maintain a tiered sensing model with gradual escalation to full PSG only in response to technician-flagged events
Deploy full PSG on all nights for enhanced resolution at the cost of participant comfort and potential data saturation
Implement an adaptive sensing strategy that adjusts the level of detail based on individual participant characteristics
levers.review_lever: This lever misses the downstream implications of sensor saturation on event-triage algorithm performance.

levers.name: Staffing Model Optimization
levers.consequences: This lever dictates the balance between staffing costs and operational efficiency. Direct effect: hiring additional research technicians can improve coverage and response times, while maintaining a lean staff might compromise participant safety.
options: Maintain a core team of experienced research technicians with flexible scheduling to ensure adequate coverage and event response
Hire additional research technicians to reduce the workload on existing staff and enhance operational efficiency
Implement an on-call system for senior staff members to provide backup support during critical periods
levers.review_lever: This lever overlooks the impact of staffing ratios on participant safety and data quality.

levers.name: Data Sharing Protocol
levers.consequences: This lever controls access to sensitive participant data. Direct effect: public sharing can promote collaboration and accelerate discovery, while restrictive protocols might limit data utilization.
options: Implement a strict permission-based system for accessing de-identified physiological data with controlled public sharing through recognized repositories
Employ an open-data policy allowing unrestricted access to all de-identified data in exchange for participant consent
Adopt a tiered data-sharing model where sensitive information is shared only within the research community
levers.review_lever: This lever misses the tension between data sharing and participant privacy.

levers.name: Event-Triage Algorithm Development
levers.consequences: This lever governs the balance between algorithm performance and manual review burden. Direct effect: more complex algorithms can improve accuracy but increase reliance on human scorers.
options: Develop a semi-automated event-triage model with adjustable thresholds for probable parasomnia episodes to reduce manual review burden
Implement an entirely automated event-triage system that prioritizes speed over accuracy and relies heavily on machine learning
Employ a hybrid approach combining automated triage with real-time human oversight to balance performance and reliability
levers.review_lever: This lever overlooks the impact of algorithm performance on participant safety.

levers.name: Budget Reallocation Strategy
levers.consequences: This lever dictates the trade-off between budget allocation for personnel costs versus equipment and participant compensation. Direct effect: prioritizing staff salaries can ensure safe operations but compromise data quality.
options: Allocate a significant portion of the budget to equipment upgrades and participant compensation to enhance data resolution and participant comfort
Prioritize staffing costs over equipment and participant compensation to maintain safe operational standards and efficient data collection
Implement a flexible budget reallocation strategy allowing for dynamic adjustments based on project progress and needs
levers.review_lever: This lever misses the tension between budget allocation and participant safety.

levers.name: Scientific Advisory Board Composition
levers.consequences: This lever governs the balance between external expertise and internal operational control. Direct effect: involving more external advisors can provide diverse perspectives but compromise operational authority.
options: Maintain a small, tightly-knit scientific advisory board with direct access to the PI for operational decisions
Expand the advisory board to include additional external experts from various fields to enhance diversity of thought
Implement an alternating membership model where external advisors serve staggered terms to maintain a balance between expertise and operational control
levers.review_lever: This lever overlooks the impact of advisory board composition on project governance.

responses.summary: Add 'independent validation through replication studies at other research sites' to Phenotyping Analysis (Aim 2).

levers.name: Participant Recruitment Diversification
levers.consequences: Diversifying recruitment channels will increase participant enrollment but may lead to a higher proportion of participants with comorbidities, requiring additional exclusions and potentially altering the study's power.
options: Expand recruitment through online forums and social media platforms targeting sleep disorder communities
Establish partnerships with local sleep clinics and private practices to refer patients directly into the study
Implement a participant ambassador program where successful completers promote the study within their networks
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between participant diversity and protocol purity, but the options overlook potential biases in online recruitment.

levers.name: Sensor Data Quality Control
levers.consequences: Implementing rigorous data quality control measures will ensure high-quality sensor data but may introduce additional costs and logistical complexities.
options: Develop and implement automated data cleaning pipelines to detect and correct errors
Establish a dedicated data quality team to manually review and verify all sensor data
Integrate data validation checks directly into the sensing tier's real-time monitoring system
levers.review_lever: Prioritizing data quality over cost carries hidden costs: none of the options address potential equipment obsolescence or software compatibility issues.

levers.name: Participant Engagement and Retention
levers.consequences: Improving participant engagement and retention will enhance study outcomes but may compromise the naturalistic environment required for accurate parasomnia capture.
options: Implement a participant feedback and suggestions system to foster a sense of ownership and investment in the study
Offer personalized support and counseling services to address participants' emotional and psychological needs during their stay
Provide participants with real-time access to their own sensor data and sleep metrics for educational and engagement purposes
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between participant experience and study fidelity, but the options overlook potential risks of participant manipulation or bias.

levers.name: Neuroscientific Advisory Board Composition
levers.consequences: Assembling a diverse neuroscientific advisory board will provide valuable expertise and guidance but may introduce additional governance complexities.
options: Recruit experts in sleep neurophysiology, computational neuroscience, and machine learning to inform algorithm development
Establish partnerships with leading research institutions and universities to tap into their expertise and resources
Invite industry representatives from pharmaceutical companies specializing in sleep disorders to provide commercial insights
levers.review_lever: Prioritizing scientific credibility over governance simplicity carries hidden costs: none of the options address potential conflicts of interest or intellectual property disputes.

levers.name: Data Sharing and Publication
levers.consequences: Fostering a culture of open data sharing will accelerate scientific progress but may compromise participant privacy and confidentiality.
options: Deposit de-identified physiological datasets into public repositories for broad access and reuse
Establish partnerships with leading research institutions to create collaborative, multi-institutional data platforms
Implement a tiered data sharing model where sensitive information is shared only with authorized researchers or collaborators
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between scientific progress and participant privacy, but the options overlook potential risks of unauthorized data reuse or misinterpretation.

levers.name: Staffing Model Optimization
levers.consequences: Optimizing staffing models will improve operational efficiency but may compromise participant care and safety.
options: Implement a flexible scheduling system to ensure adequate night shift coverage while minimizing staff burnout
Establish partnerships with local healthcare institutions to recruit experienced sleep technicians and nurses
Develop and implement a comprehensive training program for new staff members to ensure seamless integration into the study team
levers.review_lever: Prioritizing operational efficiency over participant care carries hidden costs: none of the options address potential risks of understaffing or burnout.

responses.summary: Add 'participant-led data annotation workshops' to Participant Engagement and Retention lever.

levers.name: Expansion Criteria Definition
levers.consequences: Defining expansion criteria will directly affect the facility's operational capacity, influencing participant enrollment and event capture rates. Downstream implications include potential changes to staffing ratios, sensor deployment strategies, or data analysis pipelines.
options: Establish a tiered participant recruitment model with escalating compensation levels to incentivize participation
Develop a probabilistic expansion framework based on historical participant engagement patterns and sensor yield
Implement a rolling review process for the participant selection criteria to adapt to emerging trends in parasomnia research
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between scalability and operational control, but the options overlook the impact of expansion on local regulatory compliance.

levers.name: Neuroscientific Advisory Board Composition
levers.consequences: Recruiting a diverse neuroscientific advisory board will significantly influence the research unit's scientific direction and collaboration opportunities. Downstream implications include potential shifts in publication strategies or grant applications.
options: Assemble an international advisory board comprising leading experts in sleep neuroscience and parasomnia research
Establish a rotating advisory committee with representation from multiple neuroscientific disciplines
Develop a peer-reviewed, open-access journal club to foster collaboration among the research unit's scientific staff
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between scientific breadth and depth, but the options overlook the impact of board composition on internal knowledge sharing.

levers.name: Participant Flow Strategy
levers.consequences: Optimizing participant flow will directly affect the research unit's operational efficiency and data quality. Downstream implications include potential changes to staffing ratios or sensor deployment strategies.
options: Implement a just-in-time recruitment strategy to match participant availability with sensor capacity
Develop a predictive model to forecast participant enrollment and adjust sensor deployment accordingly
Establish a participant retention program to minimize dropout rates and ensure longitudinal data integrity
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between efficiency and effectiveness, but the options overlook the impact of participant flow on local community engagement.

levers.name: Data Sharing Protocol
levers.consequences: Establishing a data sharing protocol will significantly influence the research unit's collaboration opportunities and publication strategies. Downstream implications include potential shifts in grant applications or manuscript submissions.
options: Develop an open-access data repository for de-identified physiological and sensor data
Establish a peer-reviewed, collaborative platform for researchers to access and analyze the research unit's dataset
Create a data sharing agreement template for external collaborators to ensure compliance with local regulations
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between collaboration and intellectual property, but the options overlook the impact of data sharing on internal knowledge management.

levers.name: Sensor Data Quality Control
levers.consequences: Ensuring sensor data quality will directly affect the research unit's operational efficiency and data integrity. Downstream implications include potential changes to staffing ratios or sensor deployment strategies.
options: Implement a real-time data quality monitoring system to detect anomalies and alert technical staff
Develop a tiered data validation framework based on participant engagement patterns and sensor yield
Establish a sensor calibration protocol to ensure consistent measurement across all participants
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between efficiency and effectiveness, but the options overlook the impact of sensor quality on local community engagement.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "This lever governs the tension between centralization and local autonomy, but the options overlook transition costs."
     - "Prioritizing speed over reliability carries hidden costs: none of the options address rollback complexity."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.
   - For `summary`:
     One sentence prescribing a concrete addition to a specific lever.
     Example: "Add 'partner with a regional distributor for last-mile logistics' to Supply Chain Strategy."

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach — not a vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
